scenario: RubyLLM work session - add support for a new LLM service

map_quality.rationale: Hands the agent a complete map: provider base (lib/ruby_llm/provider.rb:10-251), concrete integrations directory, shared protocol layer (protocol.rb and protocols/), configuration, errors, and even chat orchestrator — all with file:line. An agent could pick an entry point and act with only targeted lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every cited path is concrete with file:line (e.g. 'lib/ruby_llm/provider.rb:10-251', 'lib/ruby_llm/protocols/chat_completions.rb', 'lib/ruby_llm/providers/deepseek.rb:8-10'), and abstractions are tied to actual class/module names rather than vague references.

justification.rationale: Names the pieces thoroughly but doesn't really explain WHY integrations subclass a base or why wire protocols are a separate shared layer; the answer is structurally descriptive (what each file provides) rather than rationale-driven.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer reads as fully confident throughout — no hedges or callouts about layer connections, dynamic dispatch, or what a new integration must implement. Nothing flagged for the agent to verify before acting.

steps.step: Trace a request to its concrete service integration

map_quality.rationale: The step2_request_trace lays out an ordered 10-layer path from RubyLLM.chat through Models.resolve, Provider.providers lookup, Provider#complete, protocol selection, down to Faraday and ErrorMiddleware, each with file:line. Missing an explicit mention of Model::Info#provider_class as a named selection method, but Models.resolve + Provider.providers[model.provider.to_sym] covers the registry lookup the agent needs.

specificity.rationale: Methods and files are named exactly (Models.resolve at models.rb:132-156, Provider.providers via provider.rb, Chat#complete at chat.rb:295-310, Protocol#sync_response at protocol.rb:109-114) rather than vague references. provider_class isn't explicitly named, but the registry indirection is concretely cited.

justification.rationale: The trace lists each layer's action ('resolves protocol', 'instantiates protocol') but doesn't explicitly explain WHY a registry pattern is used (so new integrations are discovered by registering rather than editing routing). The why is implicit in the structure but not articulated.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer makes no flags about dynamic/load-time registry population or slug-based lookup behavior that the agent should verify. Confidence is uniformly asserted across all 10 layers without caveats.

steps.step: Map the shared API-dialect layer and its customizations

map_quality.rationale: Step3 enumerates the shared ChatCompletions base, all its mixin modules (Chat, Tools, Streaming, Temperature, Media, Models, Embeddings, Images, Moderation, Transcription), and the integrations that customize it (DeepSeek, xAI, Mistral, OpenRouter, Perplexity) with concrete override points. Missing Azure/GPUStack/Ollama subclasses called out in the rubric, but the breadth and depth otherwise leave the agent ready to inherit and override.

specificity.rationale: Files are concrete with line ranges (providers/deepseek/chat.rb:10-12, providers/xai/chat.rb:13-20, providers/mistral/chat.rb:26-33, providers/openrouter/chat.rb:11-21, providers/perplexity/media.rb:7-45), and overrides are named at the method level (format_role, render_payload, format_thinking) rather than generic prose.

justification.rationale: Step3 names the shared base as 'the class itself is empty; all behavior comes from included modules' and shows how each provider tweaks pieces, which implies the why, but it never explicitly states that ChatCompletions is the shared OpenAI-compatible dialect subclassed per-service to limit divergence.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer is presented as exhaustive with no hedging about customizations it didn't open or subclasses it may have missed (no mention of Azure/Ollama/GPUStack/OpenAI itself), so the agent has no signal to verify before copying patterns.

steps.step: Find how an integration declares and validates its settings

map_quality.rationale: The step4_settings block locates declaration (configuration_options/configuration_requirements on the provider class with OpenAI example at lib/ruby_llm/providers/openai.rb:32-44), registration (Provider.register → Configuration.register_provider_options at provider.rb:174-177), and validation (ensure_configured! at provider.rb:15, configuration_requirements check at provider.rb:159-161, ConfigurationError at 237-249), giving the agent a complete path with file:line.

specificity.rationale: Names the exact methods (configuration_options, configuration_requirements, register_provider_options, ensure_configured!, configured?-equivalent check) and files (configuration.rb, provider.rb) with precise line numbers, plus the blank-string nil coercion at configuration.rb:16-19.

justification.rationale: Implicitly explains the design (per-provider declaration registered dynamically into a shared Configuration via attr_accessor, blank strings treated as unconfigured) but doesn't make the WHY (decoupling provider settings from a central schema) explicit.

uncertainty.rationale: Does not flag where defaults, env-based settings, or per-integration optional vs required splits could vary; presents the settings model as uniformly applied without caveats.

steps.step: Trace how HTTP failures become typed errors

map_quality.rationale: Answer provides the full path with file:line: ErrorMiddleware#call entry (error_middleware.rb:14-18), parse_error method (error_middleware.rb:41-71), complete status code mapping table, the Error hierarchy (error.rb:6-105), base Provider#parse_error (provider.rb:104-124), and per-integration overrides (Perplexity, OpenRouter). Also bonus coverage of streaming errors and retry_exceptions.

specificity.rationale: Classes and methods named exactly with file:line throughout: ErrorMiddleware.parse_error at error_middleware.rb:41-71, Provider#parse_error at provider.rb:104-124, each Error subclass enumerated, and override sites cited as openrouter.rb:27-64 and perplexity.rb:26-40.

justification.rationale: Explains the shape implicitly — status→subclass mapping is enumerated and the override pattern is shown via concrete examples (HTML body, nested metadata.raw). Could more explicitly state WHY classification lives in shared middleware vs per-integration, but the architectural intent is readable from the structure.

uncertainty.rationale: Does not explicitly flag uncertainty about where new integration's error body might differ or which patterns weren't verified; lists overrides matter-of-factly without hedging on edge cases the agent should re-check.

steps.step: Find every site that must change to add a new integration

map_quality.rationale: The answer enumerates the full fan-out with file:line: registration at lib/ruby_llm.rb:104-116, registry consumers (Models.resolve at models.rb:132-156, PROVIDER_PREFERENCE at models.rb:26-40), settings registration via Provider.register, models.json catalog, and the test matrix at spec/support/models_to_test.rb:10-118 with all sub-matrices. The agent could open the PR from this list with only small lookups.

specificity.rationale: Sites are named concretely with exact file:line: 'lib/ruby_llm.rb:104-116', 'spec/support/models_to_test.rb:10-118', 'lib/ruby_llm/models.rb:11-22 (MODELS_DEV_PROVIDER_MAP)', 'spec/ruby_llm/provider_spec.rb:7-84 (api_base_cases)', plus VCR cassettes path — no vague 'wire it in' language.

justification.rationale: Each area is labeled by purpose ('Provider registration', 'Provider preference order', 'models.dev provider map', 'Test matrix') which implicitly explains why each site matters, but the answer doesn't explicitly walk through 'load-time registration vs catalog lookup vs test matrix' as distinct mechanisms — the reasoning is shown by structure more than spelled out.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer acknowledges optionality ('if needed', 'if on models.dev', 'as needed') but does not flag what static reading can miss: load-order dependencies, rake tasks that regenerate the catalog, or VCR fixture generation workflow specifically as verification risks.

map_quality.rationale: Comprehensive edit map covering all areas: new files (provider class, chat/models/capabilities/media customizations, four spec files) and edits across registry (lib/ruby_llm.rb:104-116), models registry (models.rb:11-22, 26-40), catalog (models.json), test matrix (models_to_test.rb with 11 sub-matrices), and provider_spec.rb. An agent could open the PR directly from this map.

specificity.rationale: Every entry has concrete file paths and line ranges (e.g. 'lib/ruby_llm.rb:104-116', 'spec/support/models_to_test.rb:10-24'), plus template references like 'lib/ruby_llm/providers/deepseek.rb:1-39' for each new file. No vague 'wire it in' phrasing.

justification.rationale: Each file is tied to its area (registry, dialect, settings, errors, catalog, tests) and the 'contents' field explains what each new file does. Could go deeper on WHY each edit is needed beyond labeling, but the area tagging is solid.

uncertainty.rationale: Conditional language ('if needed', 'if supported', 'optional') flags genuine variability, but the answer doesn't explicitly call out least-confident parts like fixture generation, VCR recording quirks, or which protocol customizations the unspecified new service requires.
